title: SonarApiClient.get() swallows SerializationException — silent refresh failure on malformed/empty body
description: The `get<T>()` method catches only `IOException`. A `kotlinx.serialization.SerializationException` thrown by `json.decodeFromString<T>(bodyStr)` is not caught. This happens when: (a) the response body is null (`it.body?.string() ?: ""` produces `""`), and `""` is invalid JSON for any data class; or (b) the `Content-Type` header is absent/empty (bypassing the guard at lines 526-532) but the body is HTML from a proxy/WAF. The uncaught exception propagates through `paginateSonarItems` / the `async` block in `refreshWith`'s `coroutineScope {}`, which cancels all sibling HTTP calls and propagates to `cs.launch` in `refreshForBranch`. Because `cs` uses `SupervisorJob`, the launch silently fails — `_stateFlow.value` is never updated and the UI stays on loading state indefinitely with no error surfaced.
suggestedFix: In `get<T>()`, broaden the catch to `catch (e: Exception)` (re-throwing `CancellationException`) or add a separate `catch (e: SerializationException)` block after the `IOException` handler, returning `ApiResult.Error(ErrorType.PARSE_ERROR, "Invalid JSON from SonarQube: ${e.message}")`. Also remove the `?: ""` fallback for null body and instead return `ApiResult.Error(ErrorType.PARSE_ERROR, "Empty response body")` directly.
verdictReasoning: Confirmed at /sonar/src/main/kotlin/com/workflow/orchestrator/sonar/api/SonarApiClient.kt lines 534-562. The `get<T>()` method's only catch is `catch (e: IOException)` at line 559. Lines 534-535 produce `val bodyStr = it.body?.string() ?: ""` then `ApiResult.Success(json.decodeFromString<T>(bodyStr))`. An empty string from a null body, or HTML from a proxy/WAF when Content-Type is absent or blank (the guard at lines 526-532 only fires when Content-Type is non-blank AND not JSON), will throw `SerializationException` which is uncaught. It propagates out of the `async { client.getIssuesWithPaging(...) }` blocks inside `refreshWith`'s `coroutineScope {}` at SonarDataService.kt:302, cancelling all sibling HTTP children via structured concurrency, and the job launched by `cs.launch` at line 183 silently fails without updating `_stateFlow`. UI stays on loading state indefinitely. No test covers this path. Severity is High because it silently breaks the entire Quality tab on any proxy/WAF environment that returns HTML error pages.
recommendedAction: In `get<T>()`, add a `catch (e: kotlinx.serialization.SerializationException)` block returning `ApiResult.Error(ErrorType.PARSE_ERROR, ...)`, and replace the `?: ""` null-body fallback with an explicit `ApiResult.Error(ErrorType.PARSE_ERROR, "Empty response body")` guard before the decode call.

title: Race on unguarded refreshDebounceJob/activeRefreshJob plain vars accessed from EDT and background thread simultaneously
description: `refreshDebounceJob` and `activeRefreshJob` are plain `var` with no `@Volatile` and no mutex. `refreshForBranch()` is called both from the platform-injected `cs` coroutine (default/background thread, via `subscribeToQualityScopeFlow`) and from EDT (via `QualityDashboardPanel`'s scope). A concurrent read-cancel-assign sequence can result in: (1) a freshly-created job being cancelled by the other thread's stale `activeRefreshJob?.cancel()` read, allowing two `refreshWith` invocations to run concurrently; or (2) the cancel of an in-flight job being missed because the other thread saw the old value. Two concurrent `refreshWith` calls then race on `previousGateStatus` (also a plain unguarded `var` at line 47) and can double-fire `QualityGateResult` events or fire them with wrong transition logic.
suggestedFix: Mark `refreshDebounceJob` and `activeRefreshJob` as `@Volatile`, or better: make `refreshForBranch` a suspend function and protect the job read-cancel-assign block under the existing `apiClientMutex` (it already guards the analogous check-then-act on `cachedApiClient`). Alternatively, confine all calls to `refreshForBranch` to a single dispatcher by making it `private` and exposing only a suspend wrapper. Also annotate `previousGateStatus` as `@Volatile` or move it inside the mutex.
verdictReasoning: Confirmed at SonarDataService.kt lines 60, 64 (plain `var` fields, no `@Volatile`) and line 176 (`refreshForBranch` is a plain fun with no synchronization). `refreshForBranch` is called from the EDT by QualityDashboardPanel (scope at QualityDashboardPanel.kt:53 uses `Dispatchers.EDT`) at lines 306, 404, 657, AND from a background coroutine via `subscribeToQualityScopeFlow`'s `cs.launch` block at line 145. The read-cancel-assign sequence on `refreshDebounceJob` and `activeRefreshJob` is unguarded against concurrent access from these two different threads. However, `previousGateStatus` (line 47) is only ever read/written inside `refreshWith`, which runs exclusively on the background `cs` scope, so the double-fire claim for `previousGateStatus` is weaker — but the job variable race is real and can allow two `refreshWith` invocations to run concurrently, producing duplicate or out-of-order `QualityGateResult` events. Severity is Medium (not Critical) because the EDT and background thread races on job assignment cause double-refresh at worst, not data loss.
recommendedAction: Mark `refreshDebounceJob` and `activeRefreshJob` as `@Volatile`, or confine all `refreshForBranch` calls to a single dispatcher. Annotating `previousGateStatus` as `@Volatile` is also recommended for visibility safety.

title: getLineCoverage fallback branch hardcoded to 'develop' returns wrong coverage for projects not on develop
description: When the caller passes `branch = null` (e.g. the owning git repository could not be resolved), `getLineCoverage` silently falls back to `"develop"`. Most projects use `main` or `master` as the default branch. Querying Sonar for branch `develop` on a project whose default branch is `main` returns 404 or empty coverage — all editor gutter markers go dark. The caller `CoverageLineMarkerProvider` sets `branch` from `resolver.findRepositoryForPath(...)?.currentBranchName` which can return null if the file is not in a git repo. There is no log warning that the fallback is being used.
suggestedFix: Remove the `?: "develop"` fallback. When `branch` is null, return `emptyMap()` early with a debug log (the same approach as `effectiveKey.isBlank()` on line 234). The gutter marker provider already handles `null` lineCoverage gracefully. Alternatively, fallback to the `branches` list in the current `SonarState` to find the main branch name dynamically.
verdictReasoning: Confirmed at SonarDataService.kt line 244: `val effectiveBranch = branch?.takeIf { it.isNotBlank() } ?: "develop"`. When `branch` is null (e.g. the file's owning git repository could not be resolved by the caller), Sonar is queried for branch `develop`. For projects whose default branch is `main` or `master`, this returns 404 or empty coverage — all editor gutter markers go dark silently. The KDoc at lines 220-225 documents this fallback as intentional for single-repo projects where the repo couldn't be resolved, but the comment also says 'Falls back to `"develop"` so the Sonar query still issues', which means the intention is to make a best-effort query. However, querying the wrong branch returns incorrect (not missing) coverage, which is actively misleading. The fix (return emptyMap() with a debug log) aligns with the existing `effectiveKey.isBlank()` guard pattern at line 234.
recommendedAction: Replace `?: "develop"` with an early `return emptyMap()` guarded by a `log.debug("[Sonar:LineCoverage] Cannot determine branch for '$relativePath' — skipping coverage fetch")`. The gutter marker provider already handles null line coverage gracefully.

title: SonarCoverageAnnotator uses project.basePath instead of per-file repo root — coverage lookup always misses in multi-repo projects
description: `getFileCoverageInformationString` calls `SonarPathResolver.computeRelativePath(filePath, vcsRootPath = project.basePath, projectBasePath = project.basePath)`. The `vcsRootPath` and `projectBasePath` are the same value (the IDE project/aggregator root), so `computeRelativePath` computes a project-root-relative path. But `fileCoverage` in `SonarState` is keyed by Sonar's REPO-relative path (computed by `CoverageMapper.mapMeasures` using Sonar's `comp.path` field, which is relative to the individual repo root). In multi-repo setups where secondary repos are not at the project root, the computed path includes the extra directory prefix and the map lookup always returns null. The `CoverageLineMarkerProvider` and `SonarIssueAnnotator` correctly use `RepoContextResolver.resolveFromFile` to get the repo root; this class does not.
evidence:
val relativePath = SonarPathResolver.computeRelativePath(
    filePath = vFile.path,
    vcsRootPath = project.basePath,
    projectBasePath = project.basePath,
)
val fileCoverage = sonarService.stateFlow.value.fileCoverage[relativePath]
suggestedFix: Replace the `computeRelativePath(vcsRootPath = project.basePath, ...)` call with `SonarPathResolver.resolveContext(project, vFile)` (which uses `RepoContextResolver.resolveFromFile`) to get both the correct `relativePath` and the `sonarProjectKey`. Then look up `sonarService.stateFlow.value.fileCoverage[context.relativePath]` instead.
verdictReasoning: Confirmed at SonarCoverageAnnotator.kt lines 43-47. `computeRelativePath` is called with `vcsRootPath = project.basePath` and `projectBasePath = project.basePath` — both the same value (the IDE project root). In `SonarPathResolver.computeRelativePath`, `vcsRootPath` is tried first as a prefix strip. For a file at `/project/repo_2/src/App.kt` with `project.basePath = /project`, the result is `repo_2/src/App.kt`. But `CoverageMapper.mapMeasures` keys `fileCoverage` by the repo-relative path `src/App.kt` (using `comp.path` from the Sonar API, which is relative to the individual repo root). The lookup at line 48 always returns null for secondary repos, silently disabling the Coverage tool-window column for those repos. `CoverageLineMarkerProvider` and `SonarIssueAnnotator` both correctly use `SonarPathResolver.resolveContext(project, vFile)` which calls `RepoContextResolver.resolveFromFile`; this annotator does not.
recommendedAction: Replace the `computeRelativePath(vcsRootPath = project.basePath, ...)` call with `SonarPathResolver.resolveContext(project, vFile)` to get the correct repo-relative path, then look up `sonarService.stateFlow.value.fileCoverage[context.relativePath]`.

title: SonarCoverageAnnotator passes project.basePath as both vcsRootPath and projectBasePath, making the fallback arm dead
description: Lines 43–47 call `SonarPathResolver.computeRelativePath(filePath = vFile.path, vcsRootPath = project.basePath, projectBasePath = project.basePath)`. Because `vcsRootPath` and `projectBasePath` are the same value, the `projectBasePath` fallback arm inside `computeRelativePath` is structurally dead — if the first strip attempt fails, the second will also fail identically. Additionally this ignores the actual VCS root for multi-repo projects; `SonarPathResolver.resolveContext(project, vFile)` is the canonical path that already uses the correct repo-relative root.
evidence:
43:         val relativePath = SonarPathResolver.computeRelativePath(
44:             filePath = vFile.path,
45:             vcsRootPath = project.basePath,
46:             projectBasePath = project.basePath,
47:         )
suggestedFix:
Replace lines 34–48 with:
```kotlin
val vFile = psiFile.virtualFile ?: return null
val sonarService = try { SonarDataService.getInstance(project) } catch (_: Exception) { return null }
val pathCtx = SonarPathResolver.resolveContext(project, vFile)
val fileCoverage = sonarService.stateFlow.value.fileCoverage[pathCtx.relativePath] ?: return null
```
This matches how `SonarIssueAnnotator` resolves paths and correctly handles multi-repo projects.
verdictReasoning: Confirmed real. Lines 43–47 of SonarCoverageAnnotator.kt pass `vcsRootPath = project.basePath` AND `projectBasePath = project.basePath`. In `SonarPathResolver.computeRelativePath` (SonarPathResolver.kt lines 37–38), the fallback arm only executes when the first strip fails — but since both arguments are identical, the second arm can never succeed where the first failed. This makes the fallback structurally dead. More critically, the canonical path resolver `SonarPathResolver.resolveContext(project, vFile)` already handles multi-repo by calling `RepoContextResolver.getInstance(project).resolveFromFile(virtualFile)` (line 18) which returns the correct per-repo VCS root, while this call site ignores that and passes the aggregator basePath twice. Not harmful to fix: the replacement uses the same utility already used by `SonarIssueAnnotator`.
recommendedAction: Replace the `computeRelativePath` call with `SonarPathResolver.resolveContext(project, vFile)` and use `pathCtx.relativePath` for the map lookup, matching the pattern used by SonarIssueAnnotator. This correctly handles multi-repo projects.

title: CoverageTablePanel.navigateToFile uses project.basePath, breaking double-click navigation in multi-repo setups
description: Lines 293–297: `navigateToFile` resolves the file using `project.basePath`. Unlike `CoveragePreviewPanel.showFile` (which correctly reads `owningRepo.localVcsRootPath` from `coverageData.projectKey`) and `IssueDetailPanel.resolveSonarRepoRoot`, this method hardcodes the aggregator basePath. On multi-repo projects a file in a secondary repo will never be found — `LocalFileSystem.findFileByPath` returns null and the double-click silently does nothing.
suggestedFix: Use the selected row's `FileCoverageData.projectKey` to resolve the owning repo root (same pattern as `CoveragePreviewPanel.showFile` lines 95–99). Thread `FileCoverageData` into the call site: `tableModel.getFileCoverageData(modelRow)?.let { coverageData -> navigateToFile(filePath, coverageData) }`, then resolve `basePath` via `PluginSettings.getRepos().firstOrNull { it.sonarProjectKey == coverageData.projectKey }?.localVcsRootPath ?: project.basePath`.
verdictReasoning: Confirmed real and is the same root cause as SONAR-ARC-2 (they are duplicate findings about the same code). CoverageTablePanel.kt lines 293–296: `navigateToFile(filePath: String)` uses `project.basePath` directly. The single-click handler at line 155 already calls `tableModel.getFileCoverageData(modelRow)` and passes it to `previewPanel.showFile(filePath, coverageData)` (which correctly resolves per-repo root at line 96). However, the double-click path at line 142 only passes `filePath` to `navigateToFile`, discarding the `FileCoverageData` and its `projectKey`. On a multi-repo project, files in secondary repos will not be found and navigation silently no-ops. Not harmful to fix.
recommendedAction: In the double-click handler (line 140–143), also fetch `tableModel.getFileCoverageData(modelRow)` and thread it into `navigateToFile`. Update `navigateToFile` to resolve the repo root via `PluginSettings.getRepos().firstOrNull { it.sonarProjectKey == coverageData?.projectKey }?.localVcsRootPath ?: project.basePath`, matching the pattern in CoveragePreviewPanel.kt lines 95–99.

title: CoverageTablePanel.navigateToFile uses project.basePath instead of per-repo root (multi-repo breakage)
description: CoverageTablePanel.navigateToFile (line 293-296) navigates to a file using project.basePath as the root. The FileCoverageData model carries a projectKey field (line 86 of SonarModels.kt) specifically so multi-repo callers can resolve the owning repo's localVcsRootPath, as fixed in IssueDetailPanel (F-12/F-13). However, the double-click path in CoverageTablePanel (lines 140-142) only passes filePath to navigateToFile, discarding the FileCoverageData.projectKey available at line 155. On a multi-repo project, double-clicking a coverage row for a file in repo_2 will look it up under the aggregator project.basePath and silently fail to navigate (vf is null) or navigate to the wrong file.
suggestedFix: Change the double-click handler to fetch FileCoverageData alongside the filePath: `val coverageData = tableModel.getFileCoverageData(modelRow); navigateToFile(filePath, coverageData?.projectKey)`. Update `navigateToFile` to accept a nullable projectKey and use `IssueDetailPanel.resolveRepoRoot(...)` (the same companion method used by IssueListPanel and IssueDetailPanel) to resolve the owning repo's localVcsRootPath before constructing the path. This aligns with the F-12/F-13 fix already applied to the sibling panels.
verdictReasoning: Confirmed real and is a duplicate of SONAR-CLE-7 identifying the same bug in CoverageTablePanel.kt lines 135–142 (double-click) vs lines 293–296 (navigateToFile). The double-click handler discards `FileCoverageData` (which contains `projectKey`) and calls `navigateToFile(filePath)` using `project.basePath`. The single-click handler at lines 149–161 already has `val coverageData = tableModel.getFileCoverageData(modelRow)` at line 155. This asymmetry means single-click preview works correctly in multi-repo (via `previewPanel.showFile` which resolves `owningRepo.localVcsRootPath` at line 96 of CoveragePreviewPanel), but double-click navigation silently fails for secondary repos. The F-12/F-13 fixes in IssueDetailPanel were not propagated here. Not harmful to fix.
recommendedAction: Same as SONAR-CLE-7: fetch `FileCoverageData` in the double-click handler and pass `projectKey` to `navigateToFile` for repo-correct path resolution. The two findings (SONAR-CLE-7 and SONAR-ARC-2) should be addressed as a single fix.

title: SonarServiceImpl.getInstance performs an unchecked downcast from the SonarService interface
description: SonarServiceImpl.getInstance (lines 1480-1481) resolves the service by interface (project.getService(SonarService::class.java)) and then does an unchecked `as SonarServiceImpl` cast. If a test or future code registers an alternative SonarService implementation (e.g., a stub or a mock registered in plugin.xml), this cast will throw ClassCastException at runtime. The method is not called from anywhere outside the sonar module currently, but its public @JvmStatic visibility makes it an implicit contract. The service architecture convention is that callers get the interface, not the concrete class.
suggestedFix: Either (a) change the return type to `SonarService` (the interface) and remove the cast, since no currently reachable caller needs anything beyond the interface methods; or (b) register `SonarServiceImpl` directly as the plugin.xml service key and resolve with `project.getService(SonarServiceImpl::class.java)` to make the concrete type explicit and avoid the cast. The second option matches the SonarDataService pattern (direct class registration and `getService(SonarDataService::class.java)`).
verdictReasoning: Confirmed real. plugin.xml lines 190–191 register the service with `serviceInterface=SonarService` and `serviceImplementation=SonarServiceImpl`. SonarServiceImpl.getInstance at line 1481 does `project.getService(SonarService::class.java) as SonarServiceImpl` — an unchecked downcast. However, importantly, no caller of `SonarServiceImpl.getInstance` was found anywhere in the codebase (grep returned empty). The cast is currently safe because plugin.xml always resolves `SonarService` to `SonarServiceImpl`. But the `@JvmStatic public` method is an implicit contract that could be called by tests or future code that registers a mock `SonarService` implementation, at which point the cast throws `ClassCastException`. The architectural pattern used by `SonarDataService` (direct class registration, resolved by concrete class) is cleaner. Not harmful to fix: either returning `SonarService` interface or registering by concrete class would be safe, and there are no current callers to update.
recommendedAction: Change the return type to `SonarService` and remove the `as SonarServiceImpl` cast. Alternatively, register `SonarServiceImpl` directly in plugin.xml (without `serviceInterface`) and resolve via `project.getService(SonarServiceImpl::class.java)`, matching SonarDataService's pattern.

title: SonarServiceImpl.getBranchQualityReport makes a sequential API call outside the parallel coroutineScope block (extra latency)
description: getBranchQualityReport (lines 863-1053) fires four parallel requests inside a coroutineScope block (gate, issues, hotspots, fileMeasures). After that block completes, it makes a fifth sequential call to api.getProjectMeasures(projectKey, branch, projectNewCodeMetrics) at line 1003, outside any parallel structure. This extra round trip adds 100-500ms of serial latency after the parallel phase has already finished, while the data it fetches (project-level new-code coverage summary) is independent of the parallel results and could be included in the first parallel wave.
suggestedFix: Move the `api.getProjectMeasures(...)` call inside the first `coroutineScope` block as a fifth `async { ... }` deferred alongside the existing four. Declare `val projectMeasuresDeferred = async { api.getProjectMeasures(projectKey, branch, projectNewCodeMetrics) }` inside the block, and replace the sequential call at line 1003 with `val projectMeasures = projectMeasuresDeferred.await()` after the existing `awaitAll` group. This eliminates the serial latency at the cost of no additional code complexity.
verdictReasoning: Confirmed. In SonarServiceImpl.kt, the first `coroutineScope` block closes at line 905 (after awaiting gate, issues, hotspots, fileMeasures in parallel). The call `api.getProjectMeasures(projectKey, branch, projectNewCodeMetrics)` at line 1003 is entirely outside that block and executes sequentially. This call fetches project-level new-code coverage summary, which is independent of all four parallel results and could be launched as a fifth `async` inside the first wave. Moving it inside the parallel block eliminates one full serial HTTP round trip. The fix is safe: the result is not needed until after the coroutineScope block, and parallelizing an independent fetch has no side effects.
recommendedAction: Add `val projectMeasuresDeferred = async { api.getProjectMeasures(projectKey, branch, projectNewCodeMetrics) }` inside the first `coroutineScope` block alongside the other four deferred values, and replace the line-1003 sequential call with `val projectMeasures = projectMeasuresDeferred.await()` after the block completes.

title: buildBranchQualityReportSummary truncation message hardcodes maxFilesDefault constant instead of the actual maxFiles parameter
description: `buildBranchQualityReportSummary` appends `", showing top $maxFilesDefault"` when `data.truncatedFiles` is true. But `maxFilesDefault` is the companion-object constant (20), not the `maxFiles` parameter actually passed to `getBranchQualityReport`. If a caller passes `maxFiles = 5` or `maxFiles = 50`, the summary message reports the wrong limit. The agent's LLM prompt will be told the wrong number of files were shown.
suggestedFix: Pass the actual `maxFiles` parameter into `buildBranchQualityReportSummary` as an argument, or capture it in the closure. Change the function signature to `private fun buildBranchQualityReportSummary(data: BranchQualityReportData, projectKey: String, maxFiles: Int): String` and replace `$maxFilesDefault` with `$maxFiles` at line 1204.
verdictReasoning: Confirmed at SonarServiceImpl.kt line 1204: `append(", showing top $maxFilesDefault")` uses the companion-object constant `maxFilesDefault = 20` (line 1476) instead of the actual `maxFiles` parameter passed to `getBranchQualityReport` (line 866). The call to `buildBranchQualityReportSummary(data, projectKey)` at line 1051 does not pass `maxFiles`. If a caller passes `maxFiles = 5` or `maxFiles = 50`, the summary message will report '20' regardless. The agent's LLM prompt receives the wrong count of files shown. However, in practice `maxFilesDefault` is the only value currently passed at line 866's default parameter and the agent tool always uses the default, so the incorrect message will only surface if `maxFiles` is ever overridden. Severity is Low (LLM prompt misinformation, not user-facing crash).
recommendedAction: Add `maxFiles: Int` parameter to `buildBranchQualityReportSummary` and pass it through from the call site at line 1051. Change `$maxFilesDefault` to `$maxFiles` at line 1204.

title: getLineCoverage check-then-act on ConcurrentHashMap allows duplicate in-flight HTTP requests for the same file
description: `getLineCoverage` checks the cache with `lineCoverageCache[key]?.let { return it }` and then issues an HTTP request if the key is absent. Two concurrent callers (e.g. `IssueDetailPanel.buildCodeSnippet` and `CoveragePreviewPanel.showFile`) both calling for the same file can both see a cache miss at the same instant and each fire a separate `getSourceLines` HTTP request. The `CoverageLineMarkerProvider` avoids this with a `putIfAbsent` pending-fetches guard, but `getLineCoverage` itself has no such guard. The duplicate request is wasteful and — under high concurrency — can race between `getLineCoverage` returning `emptyMap()` (error path) from one call while the other succeeds and caches the data.
suggestedFix: Add a `ConcurrentHashMap<String, Deferred<Map<Int, LineCoverageStatus>>>` in-flight registry inside `SonarDataService`. In `getLineCoverage`, use `computeIfAbsent` to register a `Deferred` for the key before issuing the HTTP request, and `await()` it if already in-flight. Alternatively, use a per-key `Mutex` map. This is the standard single-flight pattern already applied at the `CoverageLineMarkerProvider` level for the gutter-marker path.
verdictReasoning: Confirmed at SonarDataService.kt lines 238-251. The cache check at line 238 (`lineCoverageCache[key]?.let { return it }`) is a plain read from `ConcurrentHashMap` with no in-flight deduplication. Two concurrent callers that both see a cache miss at the same instant will each issue a separate `getSourceLines` HTTP call for the same component key. There is no `Deferred` registry or per-key Mutex to deduplicate. The worst case is duplicate HTTP traffic and a transient TOCTOU where one call returns `emptyMap()` on error while the other succeeds. `CoverageLineMarkerProvider` does guard this at the gutter-marker level (as noted in the finding), but `getLineCoverage` itself does not. Severity is Low because duplicate requests are wasteful but not incorrect — the last writer to `lineCoverageCache[key]` wins with valid data.
recommendedAction: Add a `ConcurrentHashMap<String, Deferred<Map<Int, LineCoverageStatus>>>` in-flight registry in `SonarDataService` and use `computeIfAbsent` + `await()` single-flight pattern in `getLineCoverage`.

title: IssueListPanel declares a CoroutineScope that is never launched
description: Line 45 creates `private val scope = CoroutineScope(SupervisorJob() + Dispatchers.IO)`. The field is cancelled in `dispose()` at line 318, but there is no `scope.launch { }` call anywhere in the file. The scope object leaks a SupervisorJob + thread until dispose fires, yet does zero work. The class is a pure UI panel with no async work of its own.
suggestedFix: Delete the `scope` field and the `scope.cancel()` call in `dispose()`. All async operations (rule fetches, navigation) are done by the injected `IssueDetailPanel` which owns its own scope, or by the platform.
verdictReasoning: Confirmed via bash grep: `scope` is declared at IssueListPanel.kt line 45 (`CoroutineScope(SupervisorJob() + Dispatchers.IO)`), cancelled at line 318 in `dispose()`, and the grep for `scope.` in the file returns only line 318. There is no `scope.launch {}` anywhere in IssueListPanel.kt. The scope object allocates a SupervisorJob and holds a reference to Dispatchers.IO until dispose fires, performing no work. This is a real dead code / minor leak.
recommendedAction: Delete the `scope` field declaration at line 45 and the `scope.cancel()` call at line 318 in `dispose()`.

title: refreshDebounceJob is a redundant alias for activeRefreshJob — both track the same Job
description: Lines 60 and 64 declare two separate `Job?` fields. In `refreshForBranch()` (lines 181–189) both are cancelled and then both are assigned the exact same `job` reference from `cs.launch { }`. Cancelling one already-cancelled Job a second time is a no-op. Both fields are always identical — one is entirely redundant.
evidence:
181:         refreshDebounceJob?.cancel()
182:         activeRefreshJob?.cancel()
...
188:         refreshDebounceJob = job
189:         activeRefreshJob = job
suggestedFix: Delete `refreshDebounceJob` (lines 60 and 181, 188). Keep only `activeRefreshJob` which was introduced by F-10 and captures both the debounce delay and in-flight HTTP children. Rename `activeRefreshJob` back to `refreshJob` if clearer.
verdictReasoning: Confirmed at SonarDataService.kt lines 60, 64, 181-182, 188-189. Both `refreshDebounceJob` and `activeRefreshJob` are cancelled together at lines 181-182, then both assigned the same `job` reference from `cs.launch {}` at lines 188-189. They are always identical — one is entirely redundant. The source-pin test in SonarDataServiceConcurrencyTest.kt at line 162 explicitly checks for `activeRefreshJob` by name, not `refreshDebounceJob`, so removing `refreshDebounceJob` while keeping `activeRefreshJob` would not break any tests. The fix (remove `refreshDebounceJob`) also partially addresses SONAR-COR-2 by reducing the number of unguarded plain `var` fields.
recommendedAction: Delete `refreshDebounceJob` (field declaration at line 60 and references at lines 181, 188). Keep only `activeRefreshJob`.

title: applyPreFilter's newCodeMode parameter is accepted but never read
description: The public method `applyPreFilter(type: IssueType?, newCodeMode: Boolean?)` (line 180) accepts `newCodeMode` but never references it in its body (lines 181–190 — only `typeIndex` and `filterCombo.selectedIndex` are set). The caller at `QualityDashboardPanel.kt:227` passes `filter.newCodeMode`, but the value is silently discarded.
suggestedFix: Either (a) remove the `newCodeMode` parameter and update the caller (simplest), or (b) implement the intent: after setting `typeIndex`, if `newCodeMode == true` call `dataService.setNewCodeMode(true)` so the filter and the toggle are in sync. Option (a) is safer unless the toggle sync was the design intent.
verdictReasoning: Confirmed real. Line 180 of IssueListPanel.kt declares `applyPreFilter(type: IssueType?, newCodeMode: Boolean?)` but lines 181–190 never reference `newCodeMode`. The caller at QualityDashboardPanel.kt:227 sets `dataService.setNewCodeMode(true)` on line 222 before calling `issueListPanel.applyPreFilter(it, filter.newCodeMode)` — so the mode toggle IS applied to the data service, but the parameter to the panel method is entirely unused/dead. This means the newCodeMode intent is partially served (the data service is updated) but the parameter itself is redundant dead weight. Not harmful to fix: removing the parameter (option a) or keeping it as a no-op is safe since the caller already handles newCodeMode separately. Severity is Low because the behavior is correct despite the dead parameter.
recommendedAction: Remove the `newCodeMode: Boolean?` parameter from `applyPreFilter` and update the caller at QualityDashboardPanel.kt:227 to drop the argument. The data service toggle on line 222 already handles the newCodeMode concern.

title: paging.copy(total = firstResult.paging.total) in combineResult lambdas is a no-op
description: In both `getIssuesWithPaging` (line 190) and `getSecurityHotspots` (line 473), the `combineResult` lambda calls `firstResult.paging.copy(total = firstResult.paging.total)` — copying the paging object and assigning its own `total` back to itself. This is identical to `firstResult.paging` and creates a needless allocation.
evidence:
190:                 firstResult.copy(issues = allItems, paging = firstResult.paging.copy(total = firstResult.paging.total))
473:                 firstResult.copy(hotspots = allItems, paging = firstResult.paging.copy(total = firstResult.paging.total))
suggestedFix: Simplify both lambdas: `combineResult = { firstResult, allItems -> firstResult.copy(issues = allItems) }` and `combineResult = { firstResult, allItems -> firstResult.copy(hotspots = allItems) }`. The `paging` field of `firstResult` is already the server-authoritative first-page envelope — no copy needed.
verdictReasoning: Confirmed real. Line 190 of SonarApiClient.kt: `firstResult.paging.copy(total = firstResult.paging.total)` copies the paging object and sets `total` to exactly its current value — a self-assignment that allocates a redundant copy. Same pattern at line 473 for hotspots. The paging information from `firstResult` is already the authoritative first-page envelope. Simplifying to `firstResult.copy(issues = allItems)` (omitting the redundant `paging =` field) is correct and safe. Not harmful to fix: the paging field retains the same value since it's taken directly from `firstResult.paging` unchanged.
recommendedAction: Replace both `combineResult` lambdas with the simplified form omitting the paging copy: `{ firstResult, allItems -> firstResult.copy(issues = allItems) }` and `{ firstResult, allItems -> firstResult.copy(hotspots = allItems) }`.

title: getMeasures has an unnecessary ifBlank fallback on metricKeys — no caller ever passes blank
description: Line 306: `val metrics = metricKeys.ifBlank { DEFAULT_METRIC_KEYS }`. The parameter already has a default value of `DEFAULT_METRIC_KEYS` (line 303). Every production caller either omits `metricKeys` (getting the default) or passes a non-blank explicit key set. There is no caller that passes an empty or blank string, so the `ifBlank` guard masks mistakes silently instead of failing fast.
evidence:
300: suspend fun getMeasures(
303:         metricKeys: String = DEFAULT_METRIC_KEYS
...
306:         val metrics = metricKeys.ifBlank { DEFAULT_METRIC_KEYS }
suggestedFix: Delete line 306 and replace every subsequent reference to `metrics` with `metricKeys`. If guarding against accidental blank is desired, use `require(metricKeys.isNotBlank()) { "metricKeys must not be blank" }` to fail loudly rather than silently falling back.
verdictReasoning: Confirmed real. SonarApiClient.kt line 303 declares `metricKeys: String = DEFAULT_METRIC_KEYS` (a non-blank default), and line 306 has `val metrics = metricKeys.ifBlank { DEFAULT_METRIC_KEYS }`. No production caller passes a blank string — callers either omit the argument (getting the default) or pass an explicit non-blank key set. The `ifBlank` guard is redundant dead code that silently masks accidental blank rather than failing loudly. Not harmful to fix: removing line 306 and using `metricKeys` directly is safe, as every real call path already provides a non-blank value via the default.
recommendedAction: Delete line 306 and substitute `metricKeys` everywhere `metrics` was referenced. Optionally add `require(metricKeys.isNotBlank())` to make accidental blank-passing fail loudly at the call site.

title: Dispatchers.Main used for Swing UI mutations instead of Dispatchers.EDT
description: IssueDetailPanel (lines 296, 374, 378, 383) and CoveragePreviewPanel (lines 115, 131) switch to Dispatchers.Main for all Swing UI mutations (codeArea.text, ruleInfoLabel.text). The project convention is Dispatchers.EDT for all UI mutations in IntelliJ plugins. While kotlinx-coroutines-swing maps Dispatchers.Main to the EDT, the codebase explicitly imports and uses com.intellij.openapi.application.EDT everywhere else (e.g., QualityDashboardPanel line 25/53, SonarDataService). This inconsistency violates the stated threading convention and will silently break if the project ever excludes the swing dispatcher artifact from the classpath.
suggestedFix: Replace all `withContext(Dispatchers.Main)` in IssueDetailPanel.kt (lines 296, 374, 378, 383) and CoveragePreviewPanel.kt (lines 115, 131) with `withContext(Dispatchers.EDT)`, adding `import com.intellij.openapi.application.EDT` to each file. This matches the explicit convention used by QualityDashboardPanel and the rest of the codebase.
verdictReasoning: Confirmed real as a convention inconsistency. IssueDetailPanel.kt uses `withContext(Dispatchers.Main)` at lines 296, 374, 378, 383, and CoveragePreviewPanel.kt uses it at lines 115, 131. Neither file imports `com.intellij.openapi.application.EDT`. Meanwhile QualityDashboardPanel.kt line 25 imports `com.intellij.openapi.application.EDT` and line 53 creates its scope with `Dispatchers.EDT`. In practice `kotlinx-coroutines-swing` maps `Dispatchers.Main` to the Swing EDT, so there is no current functional bug — Swing mutations do reach the EDT. However, the severity should be reassessed downward: this is an inconsistency/style issue rather than a real behavioral bug, because the `kotlinx-coroutines-swing` artifact is a standard dependency of IntelliJ's coroutine environment. The finding's concern about removing the artifact is hypothetical. Not harmful to fix: replacing `Dispatchers.Main` with `Dispatchers.EDT` is a safe mechanical change.
recommendedAction: Replace `withContext(Dispatchers.Main)` with `withContext(Dispatchers.EDT)` in IssueDetailPanel.kt (lines 296, 374, 378, 383) and CoveragePreviewPanel.kt (lines 115, 131), adding `import com.intellij.openapi.application.EDT` to each file. This aligns with the project convention.

title: IssueListPanel allocates an IO CoroutineScope that is never used
description: IssueListPanel declares `private val scope = CoroutineScope(SupervisorJob() + Dispatchers.IO)` at line 45, cancelled in dispose() at line 318. However, a codebase search finds no `scope.launch` or `scope.async` calls within IssueListPanel — the scope is created and cancelled but no work is ever launched on it. This is a dead resource allocation that creates a SupervisorJob and thread-pool overhead for no benefit.
suggestedFix: Remove the `scope` field and its `scope.cancel()` in `dispose()`. If future async work is added to this panel, the scope should be reintroduced at that point. Note: IssueDetailPanel (which is registered as a child Disposer and owns its own IO scope) handles all async fetches for the selected issue — IssueListPanel itself does not need a scope.
verdictReasoning: Confirmed. IssueListPanel.kt line 45 declares `private val scope = CoroutineScope(SupervisorJob() + Dispatchers.IO)` and line 318 cancels it in `dispose()`. A grep of the entire file confirms the only occurrence of `scope.` is `scope.cancel()` — there is no `scope.launch` or `scope.async` anywhere in `IssueListPanel`. By contrast, `IssueDetailPanel` (the child panel that handles all async fetches) correctly uses `scope.launch` at lines 294 and 367. The IssueListPanel scope is purely dead overhead. Removing it and its `dispose()` cancel call is safe and correct; it removes no load-bearing work.
recommendedAction: Remove `private val scope = CoroutineScope(SupervisorJob() + Dispatchers.IO)` at line 45 and `scope.cancel()` at line 318 in IssueListPanel.kt. No functional change.

title: Non-JB Swing components used for interactive controls (JButton, JCheckBox, JComboBox, JTextArea, JMenuItem/JPopupMenu) in UI panels
description: Several sonar UI files use plain Swing interactive components instead of their JetBrains equivalents: QualityDashboardPanel uses javax.swing.JButton (lines 136, 141) and javax.swing.JCheckBox (line 152); IssueDetailPanel uses javax.swing.JButton (lines 80-83) and javax.swing.JTextArea (line 67); IssueListPanel uses javax.swing.JComboBox (lines 39-40), javax.swing.JMenuItem, and javax.swing.JPopupMenu (lines 152-166); CoveragePreviewPanel uses javax.swing.JButton (line 46) and javax.swing.JTextArea (line 35). The project convention mandates JetBrains UI components only. Plain Swing components do not participate in IntelliJ's theme engine, causing visual inconsistencies (incorrect borders, focus rings, and colors) in both light and dark themes.
suggestedFix: Replace: JButton → com.intellij.ui.components.JBButton (or ActionButton for toolbar-style buttons); JCheckBox → com.intellij.ui.components.JBCheckBox; JComboBox → com.intellij.openapi.ui.ComboBox (already imported/used in QualityDashboardPanel for the repo selector); JTextArea → com.intellij.ui.components.JBTextArea; JMenuItem/JPopupMenu are acceptable as there are no JB equivalents for these. The JTextArea instances in IssueDetailPanel and CoveragePreviewPanel would additionally benefit from being wrapped in a read-only EditorTextField for syntax highlighting support.
verdictReasoning: Confirmed. Direct reads of the files show: QualityDashboardPanel.kt imports `javax.swing.JButton` (line 38) and `javax.swing.JCheckBox` (line 39), using them at lines 136, 141, and 152. IssueListPanel.kt imports `javax.swing.JComboBox` (line 39-40), `JPopupMenu` (line 152), `JMenuItem` (lines 156, 159, 165). IssueDetailPanel.kt uses `JTextArea` (line 67) and `JButton` (lines 80-83). CoveragePreviewPanel.kt uses `JTextArea` (line 35) and `JButton` (line 46). These violate the project convention of JetBrains-only UI components. Replacing with JBButton, JBCheckBox, ComboBox, JBTextArea is safe (JMenuItem/JPopupMenu are correctly exempted — no JB equivalents exist). The fix introduces no risk to functional behavior.
recommendedAction: Replace plain Swing interactive components with JB equivalents: JButton→JBButton (or AnActionButton in toolbar context), JCheckBox→JBCheckBox, JComboBox→ComboBox (already imported in QualityDashboardPanel), JTextArea→JBTextArea. JMenuItem and JPopupMenu are acceptable as-is.
